comprehensive_summary:
Heinrich von Kleist (Bernd Heinrich Wilhelm von Kleist) was a German Romantic playwright and author of novellas and short stories. He died in a murder-suicide with Henriette Vogel in 1811 (some clues incorrectly say 1815). He is a contemporary of Goethe.

"The Marquise of O" is Kleist's most frequently referenced work. In it, the title pregnant widow/noblewoman places a newspaper advertisement seeking the father of her child to come marry her. A Russian Count had earlier rescued her from soldiers during the Napoleonic Wars, only to assault her himself after she faints -- the rape is omitted from the text and represented with a dash. The groom Leopardo the chasseur is initially put up to feigning responsibility for the central action. When the Count confesses, the Marquise declares she was "ready for some villain of a fellow... but not the devil." In an incestuous moment of reconciliation, her Commandant father "hung over her as if she were his first love and held her mouth and kissed it." The Emperor appends to one law the clause "if it should be the will of God." At the end, she marries Count F.

The Broken Jug is Kleist's best-known comedy. Judge Adam, who is club-footed, tries to hide from Eve the fact that he smashed the title object (a heavy-handed metaphor for Eve's lost virginity). He claims his wig was destroyed because his cat had a litter of kittens in it, and later claims it caught fire while reading cases by candlelight. His baldness and gash on his cheek (which he explains by claiming he fell onto a sharp stove corner) become evidence against him. The secretary Licht discovers Adam's guilt. Brigitte testifies that a wig got caught on a grapevine as Adam fled. Ruprecht's conscription papers are fabricated as part of Adam's seduction attempt. Lebrecht and Ruprecht are initially blamed. Eve testifies against Adam. Inspector General Walter arrives from Utrecht. The play was inspired by a French copper engraving called La Cruche Cassee, according to Heinrich Zschokke. A woman (Marthe) gives a monologue describing the deaths of the jug's previous owners, including a Spanish invader killed by a kettle-mender named Childerich. Adam also blabbers about a noodle dumpling he plans to feed his guinea hen. Kleist also adapted Amphitryon from Moliere.

"The Earthquake in Chile" describes how Josefa goes into labor during a Corpus Christi procession and is sentenced to death. Her imprisoned lover Jeronimo Rugera prepares to hang himself but is saved by the title earthquake. They wander in a shady grove of pomegranate trees and witness "men and women of every social station" discussing "accounts of extraordinary heroic deeds." A priest compares the city to Sodom and blames its destruction on the two adulterers. The child Philip is adopted after Don Fernando is killed by a religious mob. The story is set in 1647 in Concepcion/Santiago.

Michael Kohlhaas is a novella about a horse-dealer who unrelentingly seeks justice after Junker Wenzel von Tronka mistreats his horses through a fraudulent toll. He leads a criminal army/rebellion and is eventually beheaded. Before dying, he swallows a scroll (paper listing three secrets about the future of Saxony) that predicts the fate of the Elector of Saxony. Coalhouse Walker in E.L. Doctorow's Ragtime is named in homage to Kohlhaas. J.M. Coetzee was inspired by Michael Kohlhaas to write The Life and Times of Michael K.

"On the Marionette Theatre" is an essay framed as a dialogue in which a dancer argues that marionettes can dance without being hampered by gravity. A man tells a story of fencing with a bear owned by a Baltic noble family, which could parry every attack. The essay concludes that grace appears most purely in objects with no consciousness or infinite consciousness (marionettes and God). A graceful young boy loses his grace because of his inability to exactly replicate a movement he had made with his foot. The essay ends by observing that "we would have to eat of the fruit of the tree of knowledge again to return to a state of innocence." It contrasts marionettes' movement with that of dancers.

Prince Friedrich of Homburg is Kleist's final play, not performed during his lifetime. The title character puts on a laurel wreath and receives a glove from Princess Natalie while sleepwalking. He expects to be executed for disobeying orders at the Battle of Fehrbellin but is crowned with a laurel wreath by Natalie. In the play's most controversial scene, the title character sees his open grave and desperately begs for his life. The play ends with the title officer of the Great Elector avoiding execution.

Penthesilea is a tragedy whose title character gnaws on the corpse of Achilles along with her dogs. The title character laments her inability to tell apart the words "Bisse" and "Kusse" before stabbing herself with a dagger. The play's failure contributed to Kleist's decision to commit suicide.

"St. Cecilia, or the Power of Music" describes four brothers who go mad and intone the Gloria in Excelsis every midnight after attempting to ransack a cloister in Aachen, where they were moved by a rendition supposedly directed by Saint Cecilia. "The Beggarwoman of Locarno" describes a nobleman who burns his castle after it is haunted by the ghost of a beggar woman whose death he caused. "The Betrothal in Santo Domingo" describes Gustav shooting his betrothed Toni after thinking she is a traitor when she ties him up to protect him from Congo Hoango.

Kleist is considered a major predecessor to Kafka. Franz Kafka loved Kleist and wrote that reading him "fills me as if I were an old pig's bladder." Max Brod often compared Kafka to Kleist. Susan Sontag described Robert Walser as the "missing link" between Kleist and Kafka. Walser wrote a story called "Kleist in Thun" in which Kleist "surrenders to the catastrophe of being a poet" and is unnerved by Swiss countryside beauty. Kleist also wrote the unfinished Robert Guiscard and a play based on the Battle of the Teutoburg Forest (Die Hermannsschlacht).

works.name: The Marquise of O
works.description: Kleist's most frequently clued work. A pregnant noblewoman places a newspaper advertisement seeking the father of her child. A Russian Count had rescued her from soldiers but assaulted her himself -- the rape is represented only with a dash in the text.

clues.clue: The title noblewoman places a newspaper advertisement seeking the father of her unborn child to marry her
examples: The title character places an ad in the newspaper to determine the identity of the father of her unborn child.
That story opens with the title noblewoman putting in a newspaper a request for the father of her child to reveal himself.

clues.clue: A Russian Count rescues her from soldiers but assaults her himself after she faints; the rape is represented with a dash
examples: In a story by this author, a Russian Count rescues a woman from some soldiers, only to assault her himself after she faints.
In one of this author's stories, a rape is omitted and merely represented with a dash in the text.

clues.clue: She marries Count F. who answers the advertisement
examples: In another story by this author, Count F. marries the title woman after she posts an announcement looking for the father of her child.

clues.clue: The Marquise declares she was "ready for some villain of a fellow... but not the devil" when the Count confesses
examples: One of this author's characters claims she was 'ready for some villain of a fellow... but not the devil.'

clues.clue: The Commandant father "hung over her as if she were his first love and held her mouth and kissed it" in an incestuous reconciliation
examples: A Commandant created by this author 'hung over [his daughter] as if she were his first love and held her mouth and kissed it.'
In that story, the title character's father passionately kisses her mouth when they reconcile.

clues.clue: The groom Leopardo the chasseur is put up to feigning responsibility
examples: After a man who is not Leopardo the chasseur confesses to a crime.
It is realized that the groom Leopardo had been put up to feigning responsibility.

clues.clue: Set during the Napoleonic Wars
examples: The innocent Marquise of O is violated during the Napoleonic Wars.

works.name: The Broken Jug
works.description: A comedy in which the club-footed Judge Adam tries to hide his guilt for destroying the title object while attempting to seduce Eve. His secretary Licht uncovers the truth. Adam's missing wig and various injuries serve as evidence against him.

clues.clue: Judge Adam tries to hide from Eve the fact that he smashed the title object; the secretary Licht discovers his guilt
examples: In a play by this author, Adam tries to hide from Eve the fact that he smashed the title object.
A secretary named Licht discovers that the judge he works for is behind the central crime.

clues.clue: Adam claims his cat had a litter of kittens in his wig to explain his baldness; also claims it caught fire
examples: A character lies that his cat had a litter of kittens in his wig, in order to explain his baldness.
A man explains that he does not have one of these things because his cat had given birth in it.

clues.clue: Brigitte testifies that a wig got caught on a grapevine as Adam fled; footprints of a club-footed person found
examples: Brigitte testifies that one of them got caught on a grapevine as its owner, a judge, fled.
The discovery of a wig and the footprints of a club-footed person in Marthe's garden.

clues.clue: Ruprecht's conscription papers are fabricated as part of Adam's seduction attempt; Lebrecht and Ruprecht initially blamed
examples: Ruprecht's conscription papers are fabricated as part of an attempt to seduce Eve by Judge Adam.
Lebrecht and Ruprecht are blamed for the central crime until Eve testifies against Judge Adam.

clues.clue: Inspector General Walter arrives from Utrecht
examples: The protagonist complains that a cat littered in his missing periwig shortly before the arrival of Inspector General Walter from Utrecht.

clues.clue: Adam explains a gash on his cheek by claiming he fell onto a sharp stove corner
examples: This author created a character who explains away the gash in his cheek by claiming that he fell onto the sharp corner of a stove.

clues.clue: The title object is a "heavy-handed metaphor for Eve's lost virginity"; inspired by French engraving La Cruche Cassee
examples: The title object is a heavy-handed metaphor for Eve's lost virginity.
Heinrich Zschokke stated this work was inspired by a French copper engraving called La Cruche Cassee.

clues.clue: Marthe gives a monologue about the jug's previous owners, including a Spanish invader killed by Childerich
examples: A woman describes the deaths of this object's previous owners, such as a Spanish invader killed by a kettle-mender named Childerich.

clues.clue: Adam blabbers about a noodle dumpling he plans to feed his guinea hen
examples: One of this author's characters blabbers about a noodle dumpling he plans to feed his guinea hen.

works.name: The Earthquake in Chile
works.description: Josefa goes into labor during a Corpus Christi procession and is sentenced to death. Her imprisoned lover Jeronimo prepares to hang himself but is saved by the title earthquake. A priest blames the destruction on the adulterers, and Don Fernando is killed by a religious mob.

clues.clue: Josefa/Josephe goes into labor during a procession; sentenced to death; Jeronimo Rugera prepares to hang himself but is saved by the earthquake
examples: Josefa is sentenced to death after giving birth during a procession before her execution is interrupted by the title event.
Jeronimo Rugera prepares to hang himself in prison, but is interrupted by the title event.

clues.clue: The child Philip is adopted after Don Fernando is killed by a religious mob
examples: The child Philip is adopted after Don Fernando is killed by a religious mob.

clues.clue: A priest compares the city to Sodom and blames its destruction on the two adulterers
examples: A priest compares it to Sodom and blames its destruction on two adulterers who are each saved from executions by an earthquake.

clues.clue: They wander in a "shady grove of pomegranate trees" witnessing heroic deeds
examples: Two characters wander in a shady grove of pomegranate trees after witnessing how 'men and women of every social station' were discussing 'accounts of extraordinary heroic deeds.'

works.name: Michael Kohlhaas
works.description: A novella about a horse-dealer who seeks justice after Junker Wenzel von Tronka mistreats his horses. He leads a rebellion and is beheaded, but before dying swallows a scroll predicting the fate of the Elector of Saxony.

clues.clue: Title horse-dealer seeks justice after Junker Wenzel von Tronka mistreats his horses; leads a criminal army; is beheaded
examples: He also wrote about a man who unrelentingly seeks justice after Junker Tronka mistreats his horses.
The title horse-dealer learns that his lawsuit against the Junker Wenzel von Tronka over a fraudulent toll has succeeded, shortly before he is beheaded.

clues.clue: Before dying, he swallows a scroll/paper predicting the downfall/fate of the Elector of Saxony
examples: A paper listing three secrets about the future of Saxony is swallowed by the title character before he is beheaded.
At the end of this novella, the title character swallows a scroll predicting the downfall of the Elector of Saxony before being executed.

clues.clue: Coalhouse Walker in Doctorow's Ragtime is named in homage; Coetzee's Michael K also inspired by it
examples: This novel's protagonist, Coalhouse Walker, is named in homage to Kohlhaas.
This South African author was also inspired by Michael Kohlhaas to write The Life and Times of Michael K.

works.name: On the Marionette Theatre
works.description: An essay framed as a dialogue in which a dancer argues marionettes can dance without being hampered by gravity. Features a story about fencing with a bear. Concludes that grace appears most in objects with no consciousness (marionettes) or infinite consciousness (God).

clues.clue: A dancer argues marionettes dance without gravity; their "soul" appears at a different point than the center of gravity
examples: That man also says that the title figures can dance without being hampered by gravity.
A dancer says these things are better than people because their 'soul' can appear at 'some point other than the center of gravity.'

clues.clue: A man tells a story about fencing with a bear owned by a Baltic noble family that could parry every attack
examples: A man recalls a story of how he was given a rapier and led to a chained bear, which he proceeds to fence with.
A friend tells the narrator a story about fencing with a bear owned by a Baltic noble family.

clues.clue: Grace appears most purely in objects with no consciousness (marionettes) or infinite consciousness (God)
examples: Grace appears most purely in objects that have no consciousness or infinite consciousness.
'Grace appears most purely' in these objects and 'in God.'

clues.clue: A graceful boy loses his grace because he cannot replicate a movement; essay ends: must eat from the tree of knowledge again
examples: This author recounted seeing a graceful young boy lose his grace because of his inability to exactly replicate a movement.
The essay ends with the observation that we would have to eat of the fruit of the tree of knowledge again to return to a state of innocence.

clues.clue: Contrasts the conflict between self-consciousness and grace; contrasts marionettes' movement with dancers'
examples: A writer from this country described the conflict between self-consciousness and grace in his essay On the Marionette Theatre.
That essay by Heinrich von Kleist contrasts the movement of these objects with that of dancers.

works.name: Prince Friedrich of Homburg
works.description: Kleist's final play, not performed during his lifetime. The title character sleepwalks, putting on a laurel wreath and receiving a glove from Princess Natalie. He is sentenced to death for disobeying orders at the Battle of Fehrbellin but ultimately avoids execution.

clues.clue: The title character puts on a laurel wreath and receives a glove from Princess Natalie while sleepwalking
examples: A character in one of this author's plays put on a laurel wreath and receives a glove from Natalie while sleepwalking.
Princess Natalie crowns the title character with a laurel wreath.

clues.clue: Sentenced to death for disobeying orders at the Battle of Fehrbellin; sees his open grave and desperately begs for his life
examples: This author's final play was not performed during his lifetime because of a scene where the main character sees his open grave and desperately begs for his life.
The title character is sentenced to death by the Great Elector for disobeying his orders and leading a successful attack at the Battle of Fehrbellin.

clues.clue: Not performed during Kleist's lifetime; the title officer of the Great Elector ultimately avoids execution
examples: That play ends with the title officer of the Great Elector avoiding execution.

works.name: Penthesilea
works.description: A tragedy in which the title character gnaws on the corpse of Achilles along with her dogs. She laments her inability to distinguish "Bisse" (bites) from "Kusse" (kisses) before stabbing herself.

clues.clue: The title character gnaws on the corpse of Achilles along with her dogs
examples: Along with her dogs, the title character of one of his plays gnaws on the corpse of Achilles.

clues.clue: She laments her inability to tell apart "Bisse" (bites) and "Kusse" (kisses) before stabbing herself
examples: The title character laments her inability to tell apart the words 'Bisse' and 'Kusse' before stabbing herself with a dagger.

clues.clue: The play's failure contributed to Kleist's decision to commit suicide
examples: That play's failure contributed to his decision to commit suicide alongside Henriette Vogel.

works.name: Other Works
works.description: "St. Cecilia, or the Power of Music" describes four brothers who go mad after hearing the Gloria in Excelsis. "The Beggarwoman of Locarno" is about a nobleman who burns his castle haunted by a beggar woman's ghost. "The Betrothal in Santo Domingo" features Gustav shooting Toni.

clues.clue: "St. Cecilia": four brothers go mad and intone Gloria in Excelsis every midnight after attempting to ransack a cloister in Aachen
examples: Four brothers go insane after listening to a rendition of Gloria in Excelsis that's supposedly directed by Saint Cecilia.
In a story by this author, a mother finds her four sons in a madhouse, years after their planned attack on a convent.

clues.clue: "The Beggarwoman of Locarno": a nobleman burns his castle haunted by a beggar woman's ghost he caused to die
examples: A nobleman burns himself after his castle is haunted by the ghost of a beggar woman he had earlier caused the death of.
In one of his shortest stories, a marquis is driven mad and burns his own castle down because it is haunted.

clues.clue: "The Betrothal in Santo Domingo": Gustav shoots his betrothed Toni thinking she is a traitor when she ties him up to protect him from Congo Hoango
examples: Gustav shoots his betrothed Toni after thinking she is a traitor when she ties him up to protect him from Congo Hoango.

clues.clue: Also adapted Amphitryon from Moliere; wrote unfinished Robert Guiscard; wrote a play about the Battle of the Teutoburg Forest
examples: Kleist also adapted Amphitryon, a work of unusual seriousness by this French playwright of The Misanthrope.
He wrote a play based on the Battle of the Teutoburg Forest.

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: German Romantic playwright who committed murder-suicide with Henriette Vogel. A major predecessor to Kafka. Robert Walser wrote "Kleist in Thun" about him; Susan Sontag called Walser the "missing link" between Kleist and Kafka.

clues.clue: Committed suicide (murder-suicide) with Henriette Vogel
examples: This writer, who died in a murder suicide with Henriette Vogel.
This author wrote 'The Earthquake in Chile' and committed suicide with Henriette Vogel.

clues.clue: Kafka loved Kleist; reading him "fills me as if I were an old pig's bladder"; Max Brod compared Kafka to Kleist
examples: A letter from Franz Kafka declares that reading him 'fills me as if I were an old pig's bladder.'
The diaries describe Kafka's fervent reading of this other German-language author, to whom Max Brod often compared Kafka.

clues.clue: Robert Walser wrote "Kleist in Thun"; Susan Sontag called Walser the "missing link" between Kleist and Kafka
examples: This author surrenders to the 'catastrophe of being a poet' in a Robert Walser story titled for this author 'at Thun.'
Susan Sontag billed Walser as the 'missing link' between this author and Franz Kafka.

clues.clue: Kafka's "The Country Doctor" adapts Kleist's image of emaciated horses in a pigsty
examples: Kafka's story 'The Country Doctor' adapts this author's image of emaciated horses housed in a pigsty.

cards.front: Short Story: the title pregnant noblewoman places a newspaper advertisement seeking the father of her unborn child; a Russian Count who had rescued her confesses
Short Story: the assault on the title character is represented only with a dash in the text; the Count had rescued her from soldiers but assaulted her after she fainted
Short Story: in an incestuous reconciliation, the Commandant father "hung over her as if she were his first love and held her mouth and kissed it"
Short Story: the groom Leopardo the chasseur is put up to feigning responsibility; the Marquise says she was "ready for some villain of a fellow... but not the devil"
Play: Judge Adam tries to conceal his destruction of the title object; the secretary Licht uncovers his guilt; Ruprecht and Eve are initially implicated
Play: a judge claims his wig was destroyed because his cat had kittens in it; Brigitte testifies the wig caught on a grapevine as the club-footed judge fled
Play: inspired by a French copper engraving called La Cruche Cassee; Marthe gives a monologue about the jug's previous owners including a Spanish invader killed by Childerich
Short Story: Josefa goes into labor during a Corpus Christi procession; her lover Jeronimo Rugera prepares to hang himself but is saved by the title event
Short Story: the child Philip is adopted after Don Fernando is killed by a religious mob; a priest compares the city to Sodom
Novella: the title horse-dealer leads a rebellion after Junker Wenzel von Tronka mistreats his horses; before being beheaded, he swallows a scroll predicting the fate of the Elector of Saxony
Novella: Coalhouse Walker in Doctorow's Ragtime is named in homage to its title character; also inspired Coetzee's The Life and Times of Michael K
Essay: a dancer argues the title objects dance without gravity; a man tells a story about fencing with a bear owned by a Baltic noble family
Essay: concludes that grace appears most in objects with no consciousness or infinite consciousness; we must "eat of the fruit of the tree of knowledge again"
Play: the title character sleepwalks, putting on a laurel wreath and receiving a glove from Princess Natalie; sentenced to death for disobeying orders at the Battle of Fehrbellin
cards.back: Prince Friedrich of Homburg (Heinrich von Kleist)

cards.front: Play: in the most controversial scene, the title character sees his open grave and desperately begs for his life; this final play was not performed during its author's lifetime
cards.back: Prince Friedrich of Homburg (Heinrich von Kleist)

Short Story: four brothers go mad and intone the Gloria in Excelsis every midnight after attempting to ransack a cloister in Aachen

cards.front: Short Story: a nobleman burns his castle after it is haunted by the ghost of a beggar woman whose death he caused

summary: Heinrich von Kleist (Bernd Heinrich Wilhelm von Kleist) was a German Romantic playwright and author of novellas and short stories.
